title: What is "Slop" (and why it gives me hope)
summary: Hank reflects on the word "slop" as a name for low-effort AI-generated content, arguing that our collective instinct to reject and label it, rather than passively consume it, is a hopeful sign about human taste.

title: My Big 2026 Prediction is...Not Fun
summary: John shares a sobering prediction for 2026, tied to the annual Nerdfighteria Census, reflecting on where online community and culture may be heading.

title: The Web is Going to Die
summary: In a nostalgic Thoughts From Places-style video, John reflects on how the open web is being replaced by closed platforms and algorithmic feeds, mourning what is being lost.

title: I've Not Been Doing Well
summary: Hank opens up candidly about a period of struggling with his mental and physical health, offering an honest look at not doing well.

title: This Might Actually be Happening
summary: Hank explains a scientific paper on redox-driven mineral and organic associations in Jezero Crater, arguing it could be a historically important finding in the search for evidence of past life on Mars.

title: The Problem with My Brother's Hit New App
summary: John discusses Hank's hit app Focus Friend, briefly the most popular phone app in the U.S., weighing his genuine enthusiasm against his concerns about attention and habit-forming design.

title: What #1 New York Times Bestseller Actually Means
summary: John unpacks what the phrase "#1 New York Times bestseller" actually signifies in terms of sales figures, prestige, money, and how the list is really compiled.

title: You’re Not Addicted to Content, You’re Starving for Information
summary: Hank argues that what feels like addiction to content is really a hunger for genuine information and understanding, and that the two are not the same thing.

title: What Trump's Doing
summary: John examines the early actions of the Trump administration, including the dismantling of USAID, and what these moves mean for governance and vulnerable people.

title: This Is Not Normal
summary: John considers the fragility of human progress, warning that gains in poverty and child mortality could reverse for the first time in generations amid the pause on foreign aid.

title: Do Audiobooks Count As Reading?
summary: John explores whether audiobooks count as reading, reflecting on how his own view has shifted over a 20-year writing career and what "reading" really means.

title: Fine, I'll Talk About the Drones...
summary: John investigates the 2024 New Jersey drone panic and finds there were essentially zero confirmed sightings of anything unusual, critiquing how media and the public handled the story.

title: Populism, Media Revolutions, and Our Terrible Moment
summary: Hank explores how media revolutions fuel populism and the erosion of shared trust, arguing that when trust collapses people retreat into "bespoke realities" and reality itself matters less.

title: These Maps are Lying (if you let them)
summary: Hank explains how maps can mislead through projection, framing, and data choices, and how to read them critically rather than take them at face value.

title: My Ballot Made Me Angry
summary: Prompted by his own ballot, John examines why conspiracy theories take hold, arguing they stem from distrust in systems, not understanding how systems work, and coincidences that confirm anti-system biases.

title: Is Google Training AI on YouTube Videos?
summary: Hank investigates whether Google is training its AI models on YouTube videos and what that means for creators' rights and consent.

title: Three Whole Onions with Secretary Pete Buttigieg
summary: Hank interviews Secretary of Transportation Pete Buttigieg about the work of the U.S. Department of Transportation, focusing on systems and problem-solving over political rhetoric.

title: I Believed These Four Lies
summary: Hank admits to being fooled by four misleading claims and examines how and why misinformation dupes even careful people, both in his own mind and in the systems he relies on.

title: I Quit
summary: Hank reflects on stepping back from talking publicly about the business side of his work, explaining why sharing internal operations means telling other people's stories without their permission.

title: The Weirdest Post-Chemo Symptoms
summary: Hank describes the strange lingering physical symptoms he experienced after finishing chemotherapy for his cancer treatment.

title: The Biggest Science Story of the Week
summary: Hank explains new data showing that reductions in shipping pollution inadvertently accelerated warming, revealing both that we're in more short-term trouble and that aerosols offer clues for surviving the climate crisis.

title: The Good News Video
summary: Hank celebrates news that Johnson & Johnson agreed to license generic bedaquiline for tuberculosis in high-burden countries, a major win for global TB treatment access while noting the fight isn't fully over.

title: Barely Contained Rage: An Open Letter to Johnson & Johnson
summary: In an impassioned open letter, Hank condemns Johnson & Johnson's attempt to "evergreen" its patent on the TB drug bedaquiline, arguing that blocking cheaper generics would cost many lives.

title: What to Get for a Cancer Patient
summary: Hank offers practical advice on what to give and do for a cancer patient, emphasizing genuine support, self-care items, and also caring for the caregivers around them.

title: Alone on the Cancer Boat: A Vlogbrothers Reunion
summary: John and Hank reunite to talk candidly about Hank's cancer, using metaphors like boats and micromorts to reflect on risk, expanding what healthcare can do, and how the whole experience genuinely sucks.

title: Understanding Cancer Survival Rates
summary: Hank explains how to interpret cancer survival rates, stressing that cancer is hundreds of different diseases and that population statistics don't determine any single person's outcome.

title: Rough Week
summary: John reflects on his cancer diagnosis, the role of humor in hard times, and the value of accompaniment and community as he works through a difficult week.

title: So, I've got cancer
summary: Hank announces his cancer diagnosis, sharing that a PET/CT scan shows the disease has not spread beyond its original site and that he is beginning chemotherapy.

title: Elon Was Right
summary: Hank argues that Elon Musk mistook normal public scrutiny of a powerful person for abuse, and is now wielding his ownership of Twitter to retaliate against others.

title: This "Math Test" Changed How I See Humanity
summary: Hank uses a deliberately ambiguous math test to argue that many public debates, like 'cancel culture,' are so poorly defined that they generate easy opinions and needless conflict.

title: Most People Have Never Been 20
summary: John investigates when the median human being actually lived and explores historical life expectancy, revealing surprising truths about child mortality and the human story.

title: Here's Why Younger People are Getting Shingles
summary: Hank explains why shingles is appearing in younger people, connecting it to immune system disruption and post-infection effects like those seen with COVID.

title: The Biggest Climate Bill of Your Life - But What does it DO!?
summary: Hank breaks down what the major U.S. climate bill actually does, arguing its investments will shape the country for decades in ways largely invisible to most people.

title: My Religion and My Banned Books
summary: John discusses his work as a chaplain, his complicated relationship with religious institutions, and why he writes books, insisting that book-banners do not speak for all Christians.

title: Ukraine and Russia: What Caused the War?
summary: John examines the historical and political context behind Russia's invasion of Ukraine, tracing how the two nations arrived at war.

title: The Amazon isn't "Burning" - It's Being Burned
summary: Hank argues the Amazon rainforest is being deliberately burned, and that Bolsonaro's government obscured the scale by cutting inspections and manipulating deforestation statistics.

title: What /Actually/ Happened at Chernobyl
summary: Hank explains the actual science of the Chernobyl disaster, correcting popular oversimplifications about control rods and reactor physics like the RBMK's positive void coefficient.

title: We just had our first interstellar visitor...and it's weird.
summary: Hank explores what is known about 'Oumuamua, the first detected interstellar visitor, and argues that admitting uncertainty about this strange object is more valuable than speculation.

title: Why is the Disney "D" So Weird?
summary: John examines the history and design of the Disney logo's distinctive 'D,' using it as a lens into how Disney became one of the world's most powerful cultural brands.

title: What Everyone Gets Wrong About Global Warming
summary: Hank argues that people misunderstand global warming by thinking in single outcomes rather than probability distributions, and that seeing the world through these curves clarifies climate and much else.

title: What OCD Is Like (for Me)
summary: John describes his experience living with obsessive-compulsive disorder and how it shaped the writing of his novel Turtles All the Way Down.

title: On Condom Failure
summary: Hank argues that sex ed and statistics belong together, using condom failure rates to show how easily people misread probabilities in everyday decisions.

title: The Paris Accord: What is it? And What Does it All Mean?
summary: John explains the Paris Climate Agreement and analyzes the motivations behind wanting the U.S. to withdraw, including nostalgia for coal and a go-it-alone view of national interest.

title: Some Rough Advice for the "Real World"
summary: John offers commencement-style advice pushing back against the pressure to meet every societal milestone, arguing people can be driven without punishing themselves for falling short.

title: The Adpocalypse: What it Means
summary: Hank explains the 'Adpocalypse' collapse in YouTube ad revenue and argues that independent creators need better systems and direct financial support rather than reliance on advertising.

title: Trump's Budget Explained
summary: John analyzes President Trump's proposed federal budget, noting its dramatic military spending increase alongside cuts to programs like NASA's Earth Science and social services.

title: Do I Have ADHD?
summary: John reflects on whether he might have ADHD, exploring how his personality quirks affect his relationships and his desire to understand and correct recurring mistakes.

title: Understanding Trump's Executive Order on Immigration
summary: John explains the details of Trump's executive order on immigration, its short-term effects on refugees and permanent residents, and the uncertainties that remained.

title: Why We Left Buzzfeed (Some Thoughts on Taking Risks)
summary: Allison and Gaby of Just Between Us share thoughts on taking career risks and explain why they left BuzzFeed to pursue independent work.

title: My Post-Election Thoughts
summary: John reflects on the outcome of the 2016 election and the prospect of a Trump presidency, wrestling with uncertainty while affirming the persistence of human hope.

title: Will the U.S. Presidential Election Be Rigged?
summary: John examines claims that U.S. presidential elections are rigged, walking through the electoral college and recent electoral history to assess the soundness of American institutions.

title: Understanding Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton's Tax Plans
summary: John compares the tax proposals of Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump, explaining how federal income tax works and how each plan would affect different families.

title: Failing to Follow Up The Fault in Our Stars
summary: John reflects on the years since publishing The Fault in Our Stars, explaining why no new novel has followed and what drew him back to writing after a long creative absence.

title: Why I Didn't Vote (And Six Reasons You Might Not)
summary: John examines the six reasons eligible Americans most commonly give for not voting, framing his own past failure to vote as a regret and arguing that participating in political life is essential work.

title: Understanding the Tax Plans of Donald Trump and Ted Cruz
summary: John compares the tax proposals of Republican candidates Ted Cruz and Donald Trump, explaining how each would reshape the current U.S. federal income tax system and the federal budget.

title: Clinton vs. Sanders: POLICY IS EXCITING!
summary: Hank explains and compares the tax plans of Democratic candidates Hillary Clinton and Bernie Sanders, noting Sanders' increases are tied to a sweeping healthcare overhaul, while striving to stay neutral.

title: Literally Hitler
summary: John critiques the declining quality of American political discourse, examining the fallacy of reductio ad Hitlerum and arguing that politics has become more about insults than substantive policy.

title: On the Banning of Looking for Alaska
summary: John responds to Looking for Alaska being named the most challenged book in the U.S. in 2015, defending intellectual freedom and the vital role of teachers and librarians against book banning.

title: Brazil's Government is Falling Apart...and it's Good News?
summary: Hank unpacks Brazil's Petrobras corruption scandal implicating Lula and Dilma Rousseff, arguing that citizens holding their government accountable is ultimately a hopeful sign for the country's democracy.

title: Thanks, Lemon
summary: A brief, casual Vlogbrothers video in which Hank offers thanks, keeping things light and informal rather than tackling a heavy topic.

title: Is the Gender Pay Gap Real?
summary: John investigates the gender pay gap, exploring why full-time working women earn less than men across professions, how the gap widens with age and children, and the role of discrimination.

title: Why the Word "Millennial" Makes me Cringe
summary: Hank argues that discussing generations like Millennials as homogenous groups is unproductive, and that we too readily criticize young people's flaws without acknowledging the difficult world they face.

title: Why Are Vegetarians Annoying? (An Exploration of a Cultural Rift)
summary: Hank explores the stereotype of the annoying vegetarian and the cultural rift behind it, while debunking widely cited but flawed statistics about meat's environmental impact.

title: Benghazi: Explained
summary: Hank explains the 2012 Benghazi attack and its aftermath, arguing that after eight congressional investigations the continued inquiry looks driven far more by politics than by policy.

title: Understanding the Refugee Crisis in Europe, Syria, and around the World
summary: John explains the Syrian refugee crisis and the wider surge of refugees crossing into the EU, distinguishing migrants from refugees, outlining refugees' legal rights, and noting how Alan Kurdi's death galvanized the world.

title: Understanding the Financial Crisis in Greece
summary: John traces the history of Greece's debt and deficit crisis, the constraints of adopting the Euro under Eurozone monetary policy, and the prospect of a Greek exit from the currency.

title: Why is College So Expensive?
summary: Hank examines why American higher education has grown so expensive, exploring the combination of factors driving tuition up, over a trillion dollars in student debt, and where schools actually spend the money.

title: Is Obamacare Working? The Affordable Care Act Five Years Later
summary: John assesses the Affordable Care Act five years after passage, weighing its effectiveness and repercussions and situating it within the broader, costly U.S. health care system.

title: Places I've Never Been
summary: John unpacks the widely misattributed quotation about loving cities and people one has never known, reflecting on how the internet convinces people he wrote things he didn't and on the tangled nature of online sourcing.

title: Understanding Boko Haram
summary: John explains Nigeria's complicated history and how it shaped the region that produced the Islamist terrorist group Boko Haram, along with the group's own history and recent atrocities.

title: Racism in the United States: By the Numbers
summary: John lays out racism in the United States through data, showing disparities in prison sentences, drug arrests, police stops, wrongful convictions, and juvenile sentencing between black and white Americans.

title: Hong Kong Protests Explained
summary: John explains the 2014 Hong Kong protests, exploring why China resists free elections in the territory, Hong Kong's political history, and the roots of the standoff over democratic self-governance.

title: Deserving
summary: John reflects on the role of luck versus merit in shaping people's lives, questioning what we truly deserve and how much of our fortune is beyond our control.

title: Hank vs. Hank: The Net Neutrality Debate in 3 Minutes
summary: Hank debates himself over net neutrality, laying out both sides of whether to keep the internet open or let cable companies create fast and slow lanes, and argues broadband should be reclassified as a common carrier.

title: What Boys Look For in Girls
summary: Hank reflects on initial attraction and worries that young women on YouTube tie their self-worth to appealing to boys, arguing that what people say they want rarely matches what they truly need.

title: Mass Incarceration in the US
summary: John argues that America's 40-year policy of mass incarceration is unethical and ineffective, urging viewers to see incarcerated people, many convicted of nonviolent crimes, as people rather than threats.

title: Is the American Dream Real?
summary: John examines whether the American Dream still holds, exploring equality of opportunity and socioeconomic mobility in the 21st-century United States and how much circumstance shapes success.

title: Sexual Abuse, Consent, and Culture
summary: John addresses sexual abuse and consent in the wake of troubling stories about online creators exploiting fans, discussing abusive relationship dynamics and pointing to support resources.

title: Understanding Ukraine: The Problems Today and Some Historical Context
summary: John explains the 2014 crisis in Ukraine, tracing how centuries of competing influence from Russia and Europe have shaped Ukrainian politics and set the stage for the conflict over Crimea.

title: A Middle Aged Man
summary: John reflects on realizing he is now middle-aged, and critiques society's gleeful fascination with celebrity downfalls like Justin Bieber's, asking what we should actually pay attention to.

title: Should We Raise the Minimum Wage?
summary: John examines the minimum wage debate, drawing on economic research like the Card and Krueger study to argue that raising it does not clearly reduce employment as often claimed.

title: GOOD NEWS: 14 Reasons 2014 May Be the Best Year Ever
summary: John shares encouraging global trends and statistics, from falling poverty, infant mortality, and malaria deaths to rising education and internet access, arguing 2014 could be the best year yet for humanity.

title: How to Apologize like a Fartbag
summary: Hank discusses what makes a good apology, arguing that genuine remorse for harm caused matters more than intent, and that refusing to admit fault stems from an unhealthy need to always be right.

title: On Middle School Misery
summary: Riding a train, John reflects on the misery and bullying so many people experience in middle school and why that period of life is so painful.

title: Perspective
summary: John looks back on his life twelve years ago to reflect on how much perspective and circumstances can change over time.

title: Applying for Health Insurance: Then and Now
summary: John attempts to apply for health insurance both through the Affordable Care Act's new (and glitchy) exchanges and through a pre-ACA private insurer, comparing the two experiences firsthand.

title: The Lowdown on the Shutdown
summary: Hank explains the 2013 US government shutdown, breaking down why it happened, the role of Obamacare and gerrymandering, and what the standoff meant for the country.

title: Invalid Arguments: Climate Change
summary: Hank makes the case that climate change is real, human-caused, and one of humanity's largest challenges, rebutting common denialist arguments while expressing hope that we can face it.

title: Syria in Five Minutes
summary: Hank explains the Syrian civil war in five minutes, covering the chemical weapons negotiations and arguing that regardless of diplomacy, years of bloody conflict lie ahead and refugees urgently need help.

title: Why Are American Health Care Costs So High?
summary: John explores why the United States spends far more on health care than any other nation, showing the causes are complex rather than reducible to blaming insurers, drug companies, or lawyers alone.

title: Egypt....Explained!
summary: John provides historical context for the turmoil in Egypt, explaining the decades-long tension between the military and the Muslim Brotherhood and why the country's first elected leader was so quickly ousted.

title: Inside North Korea
summary: John recounts Shin Dong-hyuk's account of life inside North Korea's Camp 14, the only known person born in the concentration camp to have escaped it.

title: North Korea: Explained
summary: Hank answers common questions about North Korea, concluding the regime is a genuine threat to global stability and especially brutal toward its own people, but unlikely to actually launch a nuclear attack.

title: Doing Things, On the Importance of
summary: John discusses the value of making things and staying productive, running through his many projects to pull himself out of a post-Project-for-Awesome slump and get back to work.

title: Why Does Congress Suck?
summary: John explains why the US Congress has become so dysfunctional, arguing that gerrymandered redistricting incentivizes most representatives to avoid serious policy work and compromise.

title: The 51st State
summary: Hank examines Puerto Rico's 2012 statehood referendum, explaining why the island remains effectively a US colony and why its path to becoming the 51st state faces steep obstacles.

title: Understanding the National Debt and Budget Deficit
summary: John breaks down the US national debt and budget deficit, explaining why the debt hasn't triggered a crisis yet but why deficits still need to shrink to keep credit cheap and global trust intact.

title: Human Sexuality is Complicated...
summary: Hank untangles human sexuality, distinguishing between sex and gender and between sexual orientation and behavior, arguing that understanding these ideas can reduce hate and self-hate.

title: What To Do With Your Life
summary: John tackles the common question of how to figure out what to do with your life, reflecting on adulthood, careers, and the meaning of it all.

title: Why The Rich Pay Lower Taxes
summary: Hank explains how US tax brackets and different income types work, and why the wealthy often pay a substantially lower effective tax rate than ordinary Americans, arguing it seems unfair.

title: Is College Worth It?
summary: John weighs whether a college degree is worth its high tuition, loans, and opportunity cost, considering both the economic returns and the intangible benefits of education.

title: An Open Letter to Students Returning to School
summary: John offers advice to students heading back to school, reflects on why public education exists, and celebrates the Curiosity rover's Mars landing as one fruit of investing in learning.

title: Religion and Gay Marriage
summary: John discusses marriage equality, examining what a traditional marriage actually is and what role, if any, theological definitions of marriage should play in its legal definition.

title: Your Arguments Are Invalid: Gay Marriage
summary: Hank argues that marriage equality is really about human equality, expressing disappointment that society still struggles to grant same-sex couples equal rights.

title: F#*%? An Explanation of Curse Words
summary: Hank explains what curse words are, where they come from, and how they function, arguing that profanity is neurologically and linguistically distinct from ordinary language.

title: Top 5 Reasons ████ ██ ██████
summary: John argues against SOPA-style internet legislation, warning that giving Congress the power to shut down websites would chill online creativity and punish casual users while missing real infringers.

title: Coming Out and Sperm Spikes
summary: Hank comes out, using quantum mechanics as an analogy to describe his struggles distinguishing gender identity from sexual orientation, after a tangent on traumatic insemination in bed bugs.

title: The Great Gatsby: Living the Dream in the Valley of Ashes
summary: John explores critical readings of Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby, unpacking its symbols including the green light, the eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg, the valley of ashes, and the American dream.

title: How to Become An Adult
summary: John reflects on the monotony and terror of one's first years in the adult working world, and how finding a mentor, like author Ilene Cooper, can help you get through it.

title: Understanding America's Debt Problem
summary: John examines U.S. public debt, the debt-to-GDP ratio, and the S&P credit downgrade, arguing the debt may be more manageable than it appears given the dollar's central role in the global financial system.

title: I Am Not a P#$$Y - this video contains mild swear words.
summary: Hank recounts being taunted while biking and reflects on bullying, arguing that bullies act from weakness and fear rather than strength, and that the goal for those bullied is to survive.

title: Your Yard Is EVIL
summary: John critiques American front lawns, noting turf grass is the nation's largest irrigated crop, is watered with drinkable water, and is inedible, while questioning its value as a carbon sink.

title: Kill the Dollar Bill!
summary: John explains the denomination effect and argues the U.S. should stop printing one-dollar bills in favor of dollar and two-dollar coins, which the GAO estimates would save billions over 30 years.

title: John Green is an IDIOT! And so are you.
summary: Hank explores human irrationality and cognitive biases, explaining why our brains systematically make flawed judgments and pointing to the long catalog of documented biases.

title: How to Turn Your Dumb Summer Job Into $162,000
summary: John explains how saving even minimum-wage summer earnings in a retirement account like an IRA can grow into a large sum through decades of compound interest, with caveats about financial aid.

title: Do You Believe in God?
summary: Hank explains why he dislikes being asked whether he believes in God, arguing the question is often used to sort people into boxes and that we should resist our instinct to categorize others.

title: On Religion
summary: John reflects on religion and nihilism, exploring how people find or reject meaning in life.

title: Baby on the Road: Thoughts from Places
summary: In a Thoughts from Places video, John reflects on driving north toward home rather than west, and on what going home, growing up, and becoming a father mean to him.

title: Probability 101
summary: Hank introduces the basics of probability, explaining how its rules help us find meaning in randomness while acknowledging the limits of predicting what comes next.

title: God and Grain: The French Revolution, Part I
summary: John explains the causes and early events of the French Revolution, from Louis XVI's debt crisis and the Estates General to the Tennis Court Oath, the storming of the Bastille, and the women's march.

title: Is Obama a Muslim? Examine the Evidence!
summary: John examines the false claim that Barack Obama is Muslim, prompted by a poll showing many Americans believed it, and along the way explains the five pillars of Islam.

title: The Greek Debt Crisis Explained in Four Minutes
summary: John explains the Greek debt crisis, why Greece neared default, why the Eurozone and IMF scrambled to bail it out, and what rising sovereign debt costs mean for developed economies.

title: I Shall BLOW YOUR MIND
summary: Hank reflects on the Hubble Ultra Deep Field image and the awe of cosmic scale, contemplating what the vastness of the universe means for our sense of place within it.

title: Health Care Overhaul Summarized Via MASSIVE PIG
summary: John summarizes the U.S. health care system and reform proposals, including the public option and nonprofit insurance cooperatives, using a pig as a metaphor for spending.

title: On Iran, Islam, and Amazingly Stupid Comments
summary: John discusses the protests against Iran's rigged election, why they matter globally, and argues that Islam, like any religion, is not a monolith.

title: Sarah Palin: An Introduction
summary: John introduces Sarah Palin, John McCain's surprise pick for the Republican vice presidential nomination, aiming to cover her even-handedly.

title: I Am Not A Pornographer
summary: John argues that his novel Looking for Alaska is not pornographic and should not be removed from the curriculum at Depew High School, defending its place in the classroom.
